title: Cat Constant Sneezing with Green Discharge: Causes and Urgent Care Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is sneezing green discharge, it likely has a bacterial infection. Learn why this is a medium-urgency situation and how to help your pet.
content_html:
What is Green Nasal Discharge in Cats?

When a cat experiences constant sneezing paired with thick green or yellow discharge, it is typically a sign of a secondary bacterial infection. While clear discharge can be related to simple allergies or mild viral irritation, colored mucus (purulent discharge) indicates that the body is fighting an active infection, often related to the feline upper respiratory infection complex, chronic rhinitis, or even a foreign body lodged in the nasal passage.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While your cat may not need an immediate emergency room visit if they are still eating and breathing comfortably, they should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If the sneezing is accompanied by a complete loss of appetite, extreme lethargy, or open-mouth breathing, the urgency increases, and you should seek care as soon as possible.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of the discharge and a short video of the sneezing episodes can be incredibly valuable for your veterinarian. Cats often experience stress at the clinic which can mask symptoms, or they may have a clean nose by the time they are examined. Providing visual evidence of the color, consistency, and frequency of the discharge helps the medical team determine the severity of the infection and the best course of treatment.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human cold medicine for sneezing?
faqs.answer: No, you should never give a cat human medications. Many over-the-counter cold medicines are toxic to cats and can be fatal. Always consult a vet for feline-specific prescriptions.

faqs.question: Is green nasal discharge contagious to other cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, many respiratory infections in cats are highly contagious. If you have other felines in the house, it is best to isolate the sick cat and wash your hands between handling them.

faqs.question: Will this clear up on its own?
faqs.answer: Green discharge usually points to a bacterial component that requires antibiotics. Without medical intervention, the infection can spread to the lungs (pneumonia) or become a chronic long-term issue.
